target.title: fix(discord): backfill missed messages after reconnect
target.body:
Replaces #81211, which was closed automatically because the author temporarily exceeded the active PR queue limit. The active queue has since been reduced.

## Summary

Fixes a Discord reliability gap where user replies can be missed when the Gateway websocket silently drops `MESSAGE_CREATE` events shortly before reconnect/resume.

The patch adds a bounded, best-effort recovery path for the common case where a user replies shortly after OpenClaw sends a Discord message:

- record recent Discord outbound channel/message anchors in memory
- on Discord Gateway `READY` / `RESUMED`, REST-fetch recent messages after those anchors
- feed recovered messages through the existing Discord `messageHandler`, preserving normal preflight, routing, replay dedupe, and session queue behavior
- add a short per-anchor cooldown so `READY` + `RESUMED` do not repeatedly rescan the same anchor
- harden reply hydration so messages with `message_reference` but missing `referenced_message` can still resolve reply-to-bot implicit mention handling

## Scope boundary

This is intentionally not a general persisted Discord history catch-up system. It is an in-memory, bounded recovery seam for recent bot-outbound channels, designed to cover the high-value "user replied after the bot spoke" failure mode without broad REST scanning.

If the process restarts, the in-memory anchors are lost; this patch does not attempt cross-process persisted recovery.

## Validation

Validated in an isolated source/stage worktree; no live gateway restart or production state was used.

- `oxfmt --check` on changed Discord files
- `oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json` on changed Discord files
- targeted regression tests:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/reconnect-backfill.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.hydration.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.startup.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.queue.test.ts`
  - result: 5 files / 68 tests passed
- full Discord extension shard:
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/discord`
  - result: 146 files / 1412 tests passed
- extensions typecheck:
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental false --pretty false`
  - result: passed

## Notes

The duplicate-delivery overlap is covered by routing both REST-recovered messages and normal Gateway messages through the same replay guard path. A regression test verifies that the same message delivered by backfill and then by the normal Gateway handler is processed once.


## Reopen/resubmit note

This replacement keeps the same branch and scope as #81211:

- Discord missed inbound recovery after gateway reconnect.
- Bounded recent-outbound REST backfill.
- Reply hydration hardening.

Validation from the original PR preparation remains applicable:

- targeted formatter/lint passed
- targeted regression: 5 files / 68 tests passed
- full Discord extension shard: 146 files / 1412 tests passed
- extensions `tsgo` passed under guarded heavy-run

No live Gateway/runtime activation was used for this PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Discord Gateway websocket reconnect/resume can miss recent inbound `MESSAGE_CREATE` events, especially when a user replies shortly after the bot sends a message. This patch adds bounded recent-outbound REST backfill and reply hydration hardening so those missed replies can be recovered through the existing Discord message handling path.
- **Real setup tested**: Isolated OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `06ff7efc51e3b591de00733361987c5770b21a59`. The after-fix behavior was exercised with a local Node proof harness using synthetic Discord/Gateway/REST seams, Discord-shaped REST payloads, READY/RESUMED reconnect entry points, recent outbound anchors, reply-reference hydration payloads, and the same message-handler/replay-dedupe contract. No production live Gateway/runtime/config/state was used, and no live Discord channel was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:


- **Evidence after fix**:

- **Observed result after fix**: The non-production reconnect scenario recovered user messages after the recent bot outbound anchor with `after=101` and `limit=50`, skipped an immediate RESUMED rescan via reconnect cooldown, filtered the bot-authored payload, dispatched recovered user messages through the existing handler in oldest-first order (`102 -> 103`), hydrated missing reply references back to the bot-authored message (`102->bot-A`, `103->bot-A`), rejected a later duplicate normal Gateway delivery of message `102` via the shared replay-dedupe path, and pruned an expired cooldown entry before processing a later anchor. Source assertions also confirm both READY and RESUMED listeners call the reconnect backfill and that monitor startup registers both listeners.
- **What was not tested**: No production live Gateway restart, live Discord channel, persisted cross-process catch-up, or broad unbounded history scan was exercised. This PR intentionally covers bounded in-memory recent-outbound recovery only; if maintainers require a true live Discord reconnect proof, that needs an explicit live-test lane or maintainer `proof: override` because this contributor-side validation avoided live runtime/config/state.

target.title: fix(gateway): bound connect frame auth.* fields at the protocol schema
target.body:
Closes #77977

## Summary

The connect-frame schema at `src/gateway/protocol/schema/frames.ts` accepted `auth.token`, `auth.bootstrapToken`, `auth.deviceToken`, and `auth.password` as unbounded `Type.String()`. The only effective cap was the 64KB pre-auth frame limit (`MAX_PREAUTH_PAYLOAD_BYTES`).

`safeEqualSecret` (`src/security/secret-equal.ts`) pads both operands to `Math.max(provided, expected)` before `timingSafeEqual` and only post-checks lengths. So a remote client could submit a ~60KB `auth.bootstrapToken` and force every stored-pairing/bootstrap comparison on the pre-auth path to allocate and constant-time-scan the inflated payload. Combined with the mutex-serialized pairing-state walk in `device-bootstrap.ts`, oversized tokens amplify per-comparison work for legitimate node onboarding.

This PR rejects oversized values at schema validation, before any handshake or secret-compare work runs:

- Add `HandshakeBootstrapTokenString` (256 chars) and `HandshakeSharedSecretString` (4096 chars) primitives. Real shared-secret/bootstrap/device tokens are 32-byte base64url (43 chars) or hex (64 chars), so 256 is well above any legitimate value while bounding the work an attacker can force on pre-auth code paths. The shared-secret cap is generous to preserve compatibility with arbitrarily-sized operator configs.
- Apply the bounded primitives to the four `auth.*` fields in `ConnectParamsSchema`: `bootstrapToken`/`deviceToken` use the 256-char cap; `token`/`password` use the 4096-char cap.

## Test plan

- [x] `src/gateway/protocol/connect-auth-bounds.test.ts` (6 tests):
  - Accepts `token`/`bootstrapToken`/`deviceToken`/`password` exactly at the documented caps.
  - Rejects each one byte over the cap.
  - Rejects a 60KB `bootstrapToken` (representative attacker payload).
- [x] Regression suite: `pnpm test src/gateway/protocol/connect-auth-bounds.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.modes.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.browser-hardening.test.ts` → 54/54 pass.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` pass on touched files.

## Notes

- Caps are well above the legitimate values (32-byte base64url = 43 chars, 64-byte hex signature = 86 chars), so no realistic client should hit them. Operators using `auth.password` for the legacy shared-secret path get 4096 chars of headroom.
- This is additive at the schema layer — no behavior change for any in-spec request.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: the connect-frame schema accepted `auth.token`, `auth.bootstrapToken`, `auth.deviceToken`, and `auth.password` as unbounded `Type.String()`. A remote client could submit a ~60KB value and propagate it to `safeEqualSecret`, which pads both operands to `Math.max(provided, expected)` before `timingSafeEqual` — amplifying per-comparison work on the mutex-serialised bootstrap-pairing walk. With this PR, the values are bounded at the schema layer (256 chars for bootstrap/device tokens, 4096 chars for shared secrets/passwords), and the ajv-compiled validator rejects oversized payloads before any handshake or secret-compare work runs.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real ajv-compiled `validateConnectParams` and the cap constants from this branch (`security/connect-schema-auth-bounds` HEAD `0b413080b8`). No vitest, no mocks. The reproducer imports `validateConnectParams` and `HANDSHAKE_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN_MAX_LENGTH` / `HANDSHAKE_SHARED_SECRET_MAX_LENGTH` directly from the patched `protocol/index.ts` and `protocol/schema/primitives.ts`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: a node runtime tsx reproducer at https://gist.github.com/fede-kamel/cbba5c994a190be7514997daf04a2d57 enumerates 12 cases — each `auth.*` field at-cap, one-over-cap, and at 60KB — and times schema validation. Run as `node --import tsx <local-copy>.mts` from a worktree on this branch.
- **Evidence after fix**: live terminal output captured from the node runtime against the patched schema:

```
Connect-frame auth bounds proof (PR 77538)
Imports real ajv-compiled validateConnectParams + cap constants
from security/connect-schema-auth-bounds worktree.

HANDSHAKE_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN_MAX_LENGTH = 256
HANDSHAKE_SHARED_SECRET_MAX_LENGTH   = 4096

case                                              | result | took
--------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: empty auth                                 | PASS   | 0.928ms
valid: bootstrapToken at cap (256 chars)          | PASS   | 0.038ms
valid: deviceToken at cap (256 chars)             | PASS   | 0.026ms
valid: token at cap (4096 chars)                  | PASS   | 0.318ms
valid: password at cap (4096 chars)               | PASS   | 0.323ms
reject: bootstrapToken cap+1 (257 chars)          | REJECT | 0.062ms
reject: deviceToken cap+1                         | REJECT | 0.352ms
reject: token cap+1 (4097 chars)                  | REJECT | 0.210ms
reject: password cap+1                            | REJECT | 0.029ms
reject: 60KB bootstrapToken (representative attacker payload)| REJECT | 0.189ms
reject: 60KB token                                | REJECT | 0.194ms
reject: 60KB password                             | REJECT | 0.200ms

=== Comparison: schema rejection vs full safeEqualSecret allocation ===

60KB bootstrapToken rejected 591 times in 100.1ms
per-rejection cost: 0.1693ms
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every legitimate at-cap value validated `PASS` and every cap+1 / 60KB attacker-shaped payload validated `REJECT`. No case produced an unexpected outcome. Sustained 60KB-rejection throughput was ~5,900 validations/sec (~0.17ms each), which is the upper bound on the cost an attacker can force at the schema layer — versus the pre-patch behavior where the 60KB payload would propagate to `safeEqualSecret` and run a `timingSafeEqual` against every stored pairing entry under the bootstrap-state mutex.
- **What was not tested**: the actual upgrade from the `ws` server through the protocol decoder is not driven by this reproducer; it exercises only the ajv-compiled validator that sits between the decoded JSON and the handshake handler. The integration shape is covered by the existing `connect-auth-bounds.test.ts` unit suite and the broader gateway server auth tests.

## Notes for reviewer

- The latest commit on this branch (`fix(gateway): split connect-frame auth caps for shared vs bootstrap secrets`) splits the cap into 256 (bootstrap/device) and 4096 (shared-secret/password) — different from the original 256/1024 in this PR's earlier description. The reproducer reads the live constants so the captured numbers track whatever is on the branch at the time.
- The cap on bootstrap/device tokens is the load-bearing one for the DoS-amplification surface; the shared-secret cap is generous on purpose because it matches a single operator-configured value and does not iterate.

target.title: fix(gateway): surface OAuth auth URLs in wizard protocol
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: RPC wizard clients could ask users to paste an OAuth redirect URL without receiving the authorization URL in the wizard protocol.
- Why it matters: Windows/remote wizard clients cannot reliably complete OAuth setup when the URL only appears in gateway stdout or logs.
- What changed: Added optional structured `step.auth` metadata for wizard text steps and wired OAuth redirect URLs through shared provider flows, OpenAI Codex, Chutes, and Gemini.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): API-key/token paste flows and existing device-code note flows remain unchanged; CLI logging/browser-open behavior is preserved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: OAuth redirect auth flows now include the authorization URL in `wizard.start` / `wizard.next` text-step metadata via optional `step.auth`, so protocol clients can show an Open/Copy URL affordance next to the redirect paste prompt.
Real environment tested: Windows development worktree for OpenClaw gateway/protocol/provider code on Node/pnpm; no live Windows companion app or real provider OAuth credentials were used.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src\gateway\protocol\wizard.schema.test.ts src\plugins\provider-openai-codex-oauth.test.ts src\plugins\provider-oauth-flow.test.ts src\commands\auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test && pnpm lint:core && pnpm lint:extensions`; `git diff --check`.
Evidence after fix: Focused protocol/provider tests assert `step.auth.url`, `step.auth.kind`, and provider metadata for shared OAuth flows, plus direct coverage that RPC wizard prompters force protocol-visible manual auth presentation.
Observed result after fix: Focused Vitest run passed 41 tests / 5 files; typecheck and lint lanes passed; `git diff --check` passed.
What was not tested: Manual Windows companion app end-to-end OAuth flow, real provider OAuth browser login, and full repository `pnpm check` / `pnpm test`.
Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Prior behavior only emitted OAuth URLs through `runtime.log` / gateway stdout while the protocol step only asked for a redirect URL.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: OAuth redirect flows logged authorization URLs to runtime output but did not carry the URL in the wizard step returned to RPC clients.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered local CLI/provider OAuth behavior but not protocol-visible auth metadata for RPC wizard clients.
- Contributing context (if known): Gateway local/remote detection is insufficient for RPC clients such as Windows/WSL, where the client needs to render the auth challenge even if the gateway environment appears local.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/protocol/wizard.schema.test.ts`, `src/plugins/provider-oauth-flow.test.ts`, `src/plugins/provider-openai-codex-oauth.test.ts`, `src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`, `src/wizard/session.test.ts`, `extensions/google/oauth.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: OAuth redirect text steps include structured `auth` metadata, shared provider handlers include provider ids when known, unknown auth schema fields/kinds are rejected, and RPC wizard prompters present auth challenges manually.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers the protocol schema, wizard prompter seam, shared OAuth helper, and representative provider integrations without requiring live OAuth credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A; new focused coverage was added.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

RPC wizard clients can now render OAuth authorization URLs from `step.auth.url` on text steps instead of relying on gateway stdout/logs. Terminal CLI users still see the same logged URLs and prompts.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
OAuth provider -> gateway runtime.log(url) -> RPC client only sees "paste redirect URL"

After:
OAuth provider -> wizard text step auth.url -> RPC client shows auth URL + paste box -> user completes login
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. The authorization URL was already logged for users; this change carries it through structured protocol metadata. Redirect URLs containing authorization codes remain user input, not `step.auth` metadata.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm OpenClaw worktree
- Model/provider: OpenAI Codex OAuth, Chutes OAuth, Google Gemini CLI OAuth seams via focused tests
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway wizard RPC protocol
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Start an RPC wizard-backed provider OAuth login flow that uses redirect-URL paste.
2. Observe the returned text step.
3. Confirm the step includes `auth.kind = "oauth-redirect"` and `auth.url` with provider metadata when known.

### Expected

- The protocol-visible text step carries structured auth metadata for clients to render.

### Actual

- Focused tests confirm `auth` metadata is attached for shared OAuth handlers, Codex, Chutes, and Gemini seams.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Protocol schema accepts structured auth metadata and rejects unknown auth fields/kinds; shared OAuth helper carries URL/kind/provider; Codex and Chutes pass provider ids; RPC wizard prompters force protocol-visible auth presentation; Gemini still forwards auth URL metadata.
- Edge cases checked: Provider field remains optional and omitted when unknown; local manual fallback reuses the last auth URL; CLI prompter remains unaffected because it ignores unknown text params.
- What you did **not** verify: Live Windows companion app OAuth UX, live browser OAuth with real credentials, full repository test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Protocol clients might accidentally depend on `auth.provider` being present.
  - Mitigation: `provider` remains optional in schema/type and tests cover omission when unknown; clients should key rendering from `auth.kind` and `auth.url`.
- Risk: OAuth URLs become visible in an additional protocol field.
  - Mitigation: These URLs were already intentionally printed to runtime logs for user action and do not contain the returned authorization code; secret-bearing redirect input is not placed in `step.auth`.

AI-assisted: Yes. Implemented with Copilot-assisted sessions and reviewed by a separate adversarial Opus 4.8 session before PR creation.

target.title: fix(gateway): cap agentRunCache to prevent unbounded growth under run fan-out
target.body:
Closes #77976

## Summary

`src/gateway/server-methods/agent-job.ts` keeps an in-process `agentRunCache: Map<runId, AgentRunSnapshot>` populated by every terminal lifecycle event. There is a 10-minute TTL pruned on every `set` via `pruneAgentRunCache`, but **no max-size cap**. Under sustained subagent fan-out (lots of `agent.run` lifecycle traffic), the snapshot map grew in lockstep with run rate up to "all runs in the last 10 minutes" before the next TTL sweep could bound it. Same shape as the Discord REST entity cache fixed in #77952.

This change adds:

- `AGENT_RUN_CACHE_MAX_ENTRIES` (default 5,000) and an `enforceAgentRunCacheMaxEntries` pass that runs after each `agentRunCache.set`, dropping oldest entries by Map insertion order until size ≤ max.
- `__testing.getAgentRunCacheSize()`, `__testing.resetAgentRunCache()`, and `__testing.agentRunCacheMaxEntries` so the cap is regression-testable.
- A regression test in `server-methods.test.ts` that drives `MAX + 25` unique end-lifecycle events through the real `emitAgentEvent` path and asserts size pins at the cap.

The sibling Maps in this file (`agentRunStarts`, `agentRunWaiterCounts`, `pendingAgentRunErrors`, `pendingAgentRunTimeouts`) are already lifecycle-bound via `start`/`end`/wait-register paths; only the terminal-snapshot cache lacked a hard cap.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts` → 82/82 pass including new `caps agentRunCache at AGENT_RUN_CACHE_MAX_ENTRIES via FIFO drop`.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-methods/agent-job.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts CHANGELOG.md` clean.
- Live tsx runtime proof included below in **Real behavior proof**.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `agentRunCache` had a TTL but no hard cap; under sustained run fan-out it could hold every run snapshot from the past 10 minutes simultaneously, scaling with run rate × 10 min and capped only by process restart or quiet periods.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22 runtime against the real patched `agent-job.ts` module (no mocks, no test framework). Driven via `pnpm exec tsx` against the actual `emitAgentEvent` lifecycle path that production code uses.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `/tmp/agent-job-cache-proof.mts` which imports the real patched module and `emitAgentEvent`, then drives `MAX + 50 = 5,050` unique terminal lifecycle events through the real listener and prints `__testing.getAgentRunCacheSize()` at intervals.
- **Evidence after fix**: live console output captured directly from the node runtime:

```
agentRunCacheMaxEntries: 5000
emitting 5050 unique end-lifecycle events...
  emitted     0  cacheSize=1
  emitted  1000  cacheSize=1001
  emitted  2000  cacheSize=2001
  emitted  3000  cacheSize=3001
  emitted  4000  cacheSize=4001
  emitted  5000  cacheSize=5000
final cacheSize after 5050 unique runs: 5000
expected ≤ 5000: PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix**: cache grows linearly to 5,000, then `enforceAgentRunCacheMaxEntries` pins it at exactly 5,000 across the remaining 50 inserts. Oldest snapshots evicted by Map insertion order (FIFO over insertion-order; on every set the new key moves to the tail, so this is effectively close-to-LRU for repeat runs and FIFO for unique-runId floods).
- **What was not tested**: behavior against a real long-running gateway with thousands of concurrent subagents. The fix is purely a `Map` cap on a deterministic insert path and the runtime demo exercises the same listener / record path that production runs through.

## Notes for reviewer

- The TTL prune (`pruneAgentRunCache`) and the new cap (`enforceAgentRunCacheMaxEntries`) compose: TTL prunes before insert, cap enforces after insert. Either alone is insufficient — TTL doesn't bound under sustained insert rate, cap doesn't free expired-but-still-inside-cap entries.
- Cap value 5,000 was chosen to mirror the Discord cache cap precedent and gives ~10 minutes of headroom at ~8 runs/sec sustained terminal events, which is generously above any realistic single-gateway run rate.
- This is part of a small audit sweep following #77952; same shape, different module.

target.title: feat(gateway): wire read coding tool into HTTP /tools/invoke (narrow)
target.body:
Related to #66561 (orthogonal — direct-invoke read benefits the embedded run-budget cascade but does not fix the underlying SSE timeout it describes; that issue should remain open). narrow first landing of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Wire the `read` coding tool into the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver) so it is reachable for deterministic automation — CI/preflight checks, lint pipelines, browser capture flows — without an LLM round-trip.

**This PR is the redesigned narrow read-only landing.** The prior 10-commit history was reset to a single fresh commit on top of current upstream `7c1a83ff2e`, restructured to satisfy ClawSweeper's [P1] from the prior review: **dual-key gating** so the existing `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` config knob does not silently grant host-FS read on upgrade.

## Dual-key gating (addresses ClawSweeper [P1])

To enable `read` on the direct-invoke surface, operators must set BOTH:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` | **NEW.** Distinct surface-specific opt-in. Without this, `read` is not materialized into the candidate set even if `tools.allow` includes it. | `false` (omitted) |
| `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` | Lifts `read` from `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` (now extended with `"read"`). Existing knob; keeps operator-acceptance step visible in the standard policy surface. | not present |

**Either key alone leaves `read` unreachable.** Old configs that already contain `allow: ["read"]` for unrelated reasons (e.g. an MCP/agent surface where `read` was always available) stay inert at the direct-invoke layer — no silent upgrade-time expansion.

| `tools.allow` includes `"read"` | `directInvoke.hostFsRead` | `read` reachable on direct-invoke |
|---|---|---|
| no  | no   | ❌ |
| yes | no   | ❌ (tool not materialized) |
| no  | yes  | ❌ (filtered by HTTP deny) |
| yes | yes  | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Read-only.** Mutating coding primitives (`write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` / `exec` / `process`) are NOT exposed by this PR.
- **Both surfaces.** Gating applies to HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND the SDK-facing JSON-RPC `tools.invoke` (they share `resolveGatewayScopedTools`).
- **MCP loopback unaffected.** `surface === "loopback"` is not gated.
- **Hook-bypass safe.** Uses new `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export (module-private Symbol marker, not reachable from typed SDK params) so HTTP-surface tools aren't double-wrapped — `handleToolsInvokeHttp` already runs `runBeforeToolCallHook` itself before dispatch.

## Files changed

- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — `SKIP_BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK` symbol + `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export
- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — `GatewayToolsConfig.directInvoke: GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` field (new type)
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — `"read"` added to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — dual-key gated materialization of `read` into candidate set
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_read_allow` finding (only fires when BOTH gates set; silent for either alone since `read` is unreachable there)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — 5 new test cases covering all 4 gate combinations + vanilla
- `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` — operator-facing dual-key opt-in docs with config example + truth table

## Code-level test coverage

`src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` (new `describe` block):

- ✅ fires (warn) when both keys set on loopback bind
- ✅ escalates to critical when bind is lan
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `allow: ["read"]` (legacy shape)
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `hostFsRead: true`
- ✅ does NOT fire on vanilla config

## Why a single fresh commit (not rebase of prior 10)

The original PR's 10 commits implemented an OLD design (single-key `allow: ["read"]` as the opt-in). The bot's P1 — "Require a fresh opt-in before enabling host reads" — needed a fundamentally different shape, not patches on top. Plus the prior commits referenced `src/agents/pi-tools.ts` which has been renamed to `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` upstream. Cleaner to reset to upstream/main and write the redesign as one commit than rebase-then-patch.

Backup of prior PR history retained at `backup/85664-pre-rebase-20260603-onto-7c1a83ff2e` on the public fork.

---

## Real behavior proof

> **Note (head `b264b15a7a`):** The live proof below ran at `0a0cafdef7`. The current head adds one docs-formatting-only commit (`oxfmt` table re-alignment of `audit-checks.md`, whitespace/dash padding) with **zero runtime code change**, so the proof remains valid for the current head.

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Expose the `read` coding tool over Gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver) for deterministic automation, gated behind a NEW distinct `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` opt-in. Addresses ClawSweeper [P1]: a single-key `allow: ["read"]` legacy config must NOT silently grant host-FS read on upgrade.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `git checkout 0a0cafdef7 && docker run --name oc-85664-proof -v $PWD:/work -v oc_proof_nm:/work/node_modules --platform linux/arm64 -e NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=12288 node:22-bookworm-slim bash /work/.build-and-proof-85664.sh` — exit 0.

Gateway started per case via `openclaw gateway run`; each case dispatched via HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK `GatewayClientTransport` WebSocket `oc.tools.invoke(...)`.
Unit tests (Docker, linux/arm64): 115 total — 10/10 (audit-gateway-tools-http) + 105/105 (tools-invoke-http). Full build + SDK build: all phases completed.

**Evidence after fix (terminal capture, head `0a0cafdef7`):** from `docker run node:22-bookworm-slim` (linux/arm64):

**Observed result after fix (head `0a0cafdef7`):** All 8 dispatches (4 cases × HTTP+SDK) from `docker run node:22-bookworm-slim` behave identically. **What was not tested:** Write/edit/exec/process are out of scope for this read-only PR (covered by #63919 for write/edit). `apply_patch` is in deny list, not yet factory-produced.

## Docs discoverability

target.title: fix(gateway): mirror hidden channel assistant commentary
target.body:
## Problem

Channel-originated sessions can subscribe to session-message events without the Control UI being visible. That hidden-session path already forwards tool events, but assistant commentary frames were not mirrored to those session-message subscribers.

ClickClack depends on those hidden session-message events to build its preamble. The result was asymmetric live behavior:

- tool calls flowed into ClickClack
- assistant commentary did not
- the final answer still arrived, so the failure looked like a missing preamble rather than a broken run

This is separate from #92092. That PR fixed claude-cli narration being dropped before gateway delivery. This PR fixes the gateway fanout path for hidden/channel sessions after assistant commentary already exists as an agent event.

## Root cause

`createAgentEventHandler()` had two distinct fanout behaviors:

1. Control-UI-visible runs: assistant events are sent through the normal visible UI agent-event path.
2. Non-Control-UI-visible runs: selected session-message subscribers get hidden live chat/tool updates, but assistant commentary events fall through and are not sent to those exact subscribers.

The hidden path was therefore complete for tool activity but incomplete for assistant commentary.

## Fix

Add a narrow hidden-session mirror path for assistant commentary events:

- only runs when `isControlUiVisible` is false
- only sends to exact session-message subscribers for the resolved session key
- only forwards assistant events that are commentary or streaming assistant deltas
- explicitly excludes `final_answer` so final replies are not double-posted
- preserves abort suppression
- uses the existing hidden `sendAgentPayload(..., { controlUiVisible: false, dropIfSlow: true })` route

## Contract documentation

This PR also adds `docs/channels/agent-event-io-contract.md`, a provider-input and channel-output contract for this boundary. It documents the normalized `assistant`, `tool`, `item`, and `lifecycle` expectations, explicitly covers delta-only assistant frames, hidden channel-session mirroring, final-answer exclusion, and channel projection into preamble/tool activity rows.

The contract is included here because the ClickClack failure exposed an undocumented seam: provider/runtime events existed, but hidden channel subscribers did not receive the assistant-commentary subset. Future provider or channel work should not have to rediscover that mapping.

## Tests

Focused gateway regression coverage in `src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts`:

- commentary with `phase: "commentary"` is mirrored to exact hidden session subscribers
- assistant streaming delta without an explicit phase is mirrored
- assistant text without a delta is not mirrored
- `phase: "final_answer"` is not mirrored
- hidden commentary is not globally broadcast
- hidden commentary is not sent via `nodeSendToSession`
- other session subscribers do not receive the event
- aborted hidden runs do not mirror commentary

Validation run:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts --maxWorkers=1

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       87 passed (87)
```

Additional checks:

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core
# clean

$ pnpm tsgo:test:src
# clean

$ git diff --check
# clean

$ pnpm docs:check-mdx
# Docs MDX check passed

$ pnpm lint:docs
# markdownlint passed
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: hidden/channel session subscribers received tool events but not assistant commentary events, preventing ClickClack from building commentary preambles for channel-originated user turns.

- Real environment tested: OpenClaw gateway built from this patch and restarted on `2026-06-11T15:31Z`; ClickClack bridge subscribed to the real channel session `agent:chisel:clickclack:channel:channel:chn_01ktq4zg9bga4dqrycqz67jts6`; real ClickClack `#test` and `#general` turns driven by ragesaq through the deployed bridge.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  1. Built the OpenClaw gateway from branch `fix/channel-hidden-commentary`.
  2. Restarted `openclaw-gateway.service` and `clickclack-bridge.service`.
  3. Drove channel-originated ClickClack turns from ragesaq in `#test` and `#general`.
  4. Confirmed ClickClack persisted `agent_commentary` rows alongside `agent_tool` rows after deploy.
  5. Captured before/after ClickClack screenshots from the real user-facing channel UI.

- Evidence after fix:

  Before this patch, ragesaq's channel-originated ClickClack turn reached the final answer, but no commentary/tool preamble rendered for the long-running turn:

  ![before: final answer without commentary preamble](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/proof/channel-hidden-commentary-evidence/.proof/channel-hidden-commentary/before-real-user-channel-proof.png)

  After this patch, ragesaq's channel-originated ClickClack turn renders an expanded preamble with assistant commentary interleaved with tool-call rows:

  ![after: expanded preamble with commentary and tool calls](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/proof/channel-hidden-commentary-evidence/.proof/channel-hidden-commentary/after-real-user-channel-proof.png)

  ClickClack also persisted real `agent_commentary` rows in the channel alongside `agent_tool` rows. Example rows from the ClickClack DB after deploy:

```text
2026-06-11T15:55:29.371130227Z  agent_commentary  Pushed (`6055c05`). Posting the summary to the channel.
2026-06-11T15:55:21.602563769Z  agent_tool        **exec**
2026-06-11T15:55:41.92467386Z   agent_tool        **message**
```

  Additional user-visible proof screenshots are committed on fork branch `proof/channel-hidden-commentary-evidence`:

  - expanded preamble, commentary interleaved with tool-call rows: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/proof/channel-hidden-commentary-evidence/.proof/channel-hidden-commentary/preamble-expanded-commentary-tools.png
  - collapsed preamble with final answer still visible: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/proof/channel-hidden-commentary-evidence/.proof/channel-hidden-commentary/preamble-collapsed-final-visible.png
  - proof note: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ragesaq/openclaw/proof/channel-hidden-commentary-evidence/.proof/channel-hidden-commentary/real-behavior-proof.md

- Observed result after fix: ClickClack receives hidden assistant commentary frames for the exact channel session, persists them as durable `agent_commentary` messages, and renders a user-visible preamble containing commentary plus tool-call rows while the final answer remains visible.

- What was not tested: upstream-maintainer-hosted deployment. This PR also does not change or test ClickClack rendering internals; it fixes the OpenClaw gateway fanout path that ClickClack already consumes.

## Risk checklist

- **Behavior change scope:** hidden/channel session-message subscribers now receive assistant commentary events they were already subscribed to but missing. Control-UI-visible behavior is untouched.
- **Final-answer duplication:** explicitly guarded by excluding `phase === "final_answer"`.
- **Session isolation:** uses the existing exact session-message subscriber registry and test asserts only the selected session receives events.
- **Abort behavior:** preserved and covered by regression test.
- **Persistence/schema:** no schema or storage changes.
- **Backpressure:** uses the existing hidden subscriber `dropIfSlow: true` behavior.

## Not in scope

- ClickClack rendering implementation. This PR fixes the OpenClaw gateway event fanout path only.
- #92092's claude-cli parser lane fix. That bug was upstream of this path and has already merged.

## AI assistance

AI-assisted. Authored under ragesaq's OpenClaw contributor identity with Forge and Chisel assistance. ragesaq supplied the real before/after ClickClack screenshots and directed the proof update; Chisel prepared this PR body/proof revision with `openai/gpt-5.5`.

target.title: fix(plugins): keep CLI auth backends out of startup agent harnesses
target.body:
## Summary

CLI backends declared in a plugin manifest's `cliBackends` (e.g. Anthropic's `claude-cli`) are **auth backends**, not agent harness runtimes. The installed-plugin-index record builder was folding them into `startup.agentHarnesses`, so startup planning treated a login backend as a required harness and then failed harness selection with:

```
Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.
```

This PR limits `startup.agentHarnesses` to manifest-declared `activation.onAgentHarnesses` only.

- `src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-record-builder.ts`: stop folding `record.cliBackends` into the startup harness list (keeps the `normalizeSortedUniqueStringEntries` helper and `configPaths` field from current `main`).
- `src/plugins/installed-plugin-index.test.ts`: regression assertion that a CLI backend (`demo-cli`) does not leak into `startup.agentHarnesses`.
- `Dockerfile`: optional `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_CLAUDE_CLI` build arg to install the Claude Code CLI with a pinned SHA-512 integrity check (verified against `npm view dist.integrity` before install), enabling the `claude-cli` agent runtime inside the container.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: Fixes + Changes entries.

## Verification

Local, on this branch rebased onto latest `origin/main`:

```
pnpm test src/plugins/installed-plugin-index.test.ts   # 30/30 passed
pnpm tsgo                                              # exit 0
pnpm check:changed                                     # oxlint clean, 0 runtime import cycles, all guards pass
```

Real after-fix behavior proof (live Docker gateway, 2026-06-12):

- Before: a gateway container running the `anthropic` plugin with `claude-cli` as a CLI auth backend failed startup harness selection with `Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered`.
- After: rebuilt the image from this branch (`docker compose build` with `--build-arg OPENCLAW_INSTALL_CLAUDE_CLI=1`) and recreated the container. Result: container healthy, **zero** `error`/`fail` lines in startup logs, the "not registered" harness error is gone, and `claude --version` inside the container reports `2.1.154 (Claude Code)` — matching the pinned integrity version.

target.title: fix(model-fallback): don't rethrow provider-side AbortErrors as user cancellations
target.body:
## Problem

When the LLM API closes the connection mid-stream, the fetch layer surfaces `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` with **no external abort signal triggered** (`"externalAbort: false"` in trajectory). The existing guard `shouldRethrowAbort()` returned `false` for these errors (because `isTimeoutError` matched the "operation was aborted" message), so they fell through to the fallback loop but were never retried — the error propagated up and produced `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN` in group/channel sessions, permanently silencing the topic.

## Fix

1. **model-fallback.ts**: Add an explicit guard **before** `coerceToFailoverError` in `runFallbackCandidate`: if the error is an `AbortError` and the external abort signal is already aborted, rethrow immediately (user/gateway cancellation). Provider-side `AbortError`s (no external signal) fall through to the next fallback candidate.

```typescript
// Before coerceToFailoverError normalization:
if (isFallbackAbortError(err) && params.abortSignal?.aborted) {
  throw err;
}
```

2. **compact.ts**: Thread `params.abortSignal` into the `runWithModelFallback` call so compaction cancellations are correctly forwarded to the new guard.

Also removes the unused `shouldRethrowAbort` helper and `isTimeoutError` import.

## Scope

- **3 files changed**, +66 / -8
- `model-fallback.ts`: guard moved before normalization (core fix)
- `compact.ts`: abortSignal forwarding (P2 fix)
- `model-fallback.test.ts`: 3 regression tests

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Provider-side `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` (no external abort signal) was misclassified by `shouldRethrowAbort()` → `isTimeoutError()` matching the message, causing the fallback loop to silently swallow the error. In group/channel sessions this produced `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`, permanently silencing the topic with zero visible response.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS (Apple Silicon), Node v22, patched OpenClaw dist at `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/model-fallback--w47Y_pS.js`. Gateway restarted with patched dist. Guard logic verified against the actual production module code.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# 1. Patch the dist file with the fix
# 2. Restart the gateway
launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/ai.openclaw.gateway

# 3. Run the proof script against the patched dist
node /tmp/real-behavior-proof-v2.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Patched dist verification ===
File exists: true
Size: 50112 bytes
New guard (isFallbackAbortError && abortSignal.aborted): PRESENT ✓
Old guard (shouldRethrowAbort && !normalizedFailover): REMOVED ✓

=== Guard behavior (patched code) ===
Provider-side AbortError (no signal): FALLTHROUGH (provider abort → retry) ✓
User cancel (AbortError reason):     RETHROW (user cancellation) ✓
User cancel (TimeoutError reason):   RETHROW (user cancellation) ✓

=== Compact abortSignal forwarding ===
compact.ts passes abortSignal to runWithModelFallback: YES ✓

=== RESULT: ALL CORRECT ✅ ===
Provider-side abort → fallback retry (topic recovers)
User cancellation → immediate stop (no wasted provider calls)
Compact caller → abortSignal forwarded (no silent compaction retries)
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Provider-side AbortError (no signal): guard does NOT fire → error enters `coerceToFailoverError` → falls through to next fallback candidate → topic recovers
  - User cancel (AbortError reason): guard fires (`abortSignal.aborted = true`) → rethrows immediately → no wasted provider calls
  - User cancel (TimeoutError reason): `isTerminalAbort` catches first → rethrows immediately
  - Compact caller: `abortSignal` forwarded to `runWithModelFallback` → new guard correctly stops on user cancellation
  - All 79 model-fallback tests pass, including 3 new regression tests

- **What was not tested**: Live gateway end-to-end with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction (guard logic verified against production dist module; full network-layer round-trip not driven). `agent-runner-execution.ts` SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN routing (separate follow-up if needed).

target.title: fix(reply): suppress direct tool-error progress leaks
target.body:
## Summary
- suppress text-only tool error progress in direct chats when the turn still produces a normal final reply
- keep the existing terminal fallback path intact so real failures still surface when there is no usable final answer
- add a regression test covering the reproduced Telegram DM cold-start leak

## Repro / proof
Concrete live repro from Joseph's Telegram DM on **June 3, 2026** after the machine had been off for about a week:

1. Power the system back on.
2. Start a direct Telegram conversation with a lightweight greeting like `Yo`.
3. The agent startup sequence tries to read expected daily memory files for today/yesterday.
4. If one of those files does not exist, the internal tool failure is emitted into the DM before or alongside the real reply.

Observed visible output:
- user: `Yo`
- assistant final reply: `Yo.`
- leaked tool-progress error: `⚠️ 🛠️ print lines 1-220 from memory/2026-06-03.md (agent) failed`

That is the wrong surface behavior for a trivial greeting. The user should get the greeting, and any internal failed read should stay internal unless the turn actually ends in failure.

## Root cause
`dispatch-from-config` was forwarding text-only `onToolResult(... isError: true ...)` payloads directly to user-visible delivery in direct chats. That bypassed the later terminal-warning policy, which already tries to avoid showing warning text when a normal reply exists.

## Fix
This change suppresses direct-chat text-only tool error progress payloads before delivery, while preserving the existing end-of-turn fallback logic. The result is:
- normal direct replies stay clean
- tool failures can still surface at the end of a truly failed turn
- media/approval payloads are unaffected
- verbose `full` mode behavior is unchanged

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Direct Telegram chats could leak text-only internal tool-error progress, such as a missing startup memory-file read, before/alongside a normal final reply. This patch keeps the normal final reply visible while suppressing that raw internal progress line.
- **Real environment tested**: Exact PR head `1579b5197f3938f6ec904f3e319c7514e7f88291` checked out from `jewseppi/openclaw` in `/tmp/openclaw-pr89975-proof.6sEupH/repo` on Joseph's OpenClaw host, using `pnpm 11.2.2` and the repository lockfile. A live Telegram Bot API dispatcher was wired into that exact-head `dispatchReplyFromConfig` path and sent to Joseph's real Telegram DM (`chat_id=6300969793`). The live Telegram direct conversation also reproduced the same missing-memory-file internal failure mode during this proof attempt without surfacing the raw `sed: can't read memory/2026-06-06.md` / `memory/2026-06-05.md` tool output as Telegram chat text.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash

pnpm test -- --run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts

```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from the exact-head checkout and live Telegram dispatch probe:

```text

```

- **Observed result after fix**: The exact-head live Telegram dispatcher attempted the failed progress payload `⚠️ 🛠️ PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z print lines 1-220 from memory/2026-06-03.md (agent) failed`, but sent only the final Telegram message `PR89975-LIVE-PROOF-2026-06-07T02-41-20-545Z final reply: Yo.` as Telegram message id `4201`. The exact-head regression coverage also proves the direct-chat dispatch path drops the text-only `isError` tool-progress payload and still sends the normal final reply (`Yo.`), while the embedded-runner coverage preserves real terminal warning fallback behavior for unacknowledged/unsafe failures.
- **What was not tested**: I could not produce maintainer-owned native Telegram Desktop Mantis video from this account. `workflow_dispatch` for `mantis-telegram-desktop-proof.yml` is repository-admin-only, and previous comment-trigger attempts were skipped/unauthorized because `jewseppi` only has read-level access on `openclaw/openclaw`.

## Test
- `pnpm test -- --run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`

## Related evidence
This came up during a broader cold-start recovery path after the machine had been powered off for days. There was also a separate stale-`dist` cold-start failure reported earlier in the week. That looks like a different root cause; this PR only fixes the leaked internal tool-error symptom above.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Channel echo / session pinning (pin-from-here) with native Telegram rendering A session can mirror its turns to other threads that are bound to the **same** session, so a long run you started in one place can be followed from another — and the mirror renders **natively** in the destination channel (live draft stream, not a flat `[echo]` copy). ### What changed since the earlier revision (maintainer feedback) The earlier revision let a session pin **arbitrary** `{channel, to, threadId}` echo targets. The maintainer's concern was that continuous mirroring to arbitrary chat ids is a heavy primitive and lacks an authorization story for streaming conversation content to any chat id. This revision reshapes the opt-in surface to **pin-from-here**, which removes that surface entirely: - A thread opts **itself** in with the in-chat command **`/pin on`** (alias `/mirror`). It can only mirror the session it is **already bound to** — there is no arbitrary-target argument — so a pin can never cross a session boundary. Every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that exact session. **Zero new authorization surface.** - The operator add paths (`sessions echo` CLI + `sessions.echo` RPC) now **reject any target that is not a verified participant** of the session (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), with guidance to use `/pin` from the target thread instead. The "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path is gone. - Self-exclusion is correct by construction: `/pin` captures the thread's own origin identity (the exact fields the fan-out uses as a turn's origin), so when this thread drives a turn it is excluded from its own fan-out; when a sibling thread of the same session drives, this thread receives the mirror. `last*` (the session's persistent external delivery route) is unchanged — a transient webchat/cron poke still must not hijack an external session's route (session-delivery.ts, #47745). ### Native rendering for every origin The native streaming renderer replays the origin run's agent-event bus into each pinned channel's own compositor (one agent run, N native renders), so the mirror carries the destination channel's real rendering rather than a flat final. **The mirror rides the destination thread's own streaming config** — it renders the turn as if it had happened natively in that thread, not a stripped subset. The mirror resolver already replays the origin run's full event stream (`assistant`/`item`/`tool`/`reasoning`/`plan`/`command`/`patch`) into the target's `GetReplyOptions`, but the Telegram echo renderer previously implemented only `onPartialReply`, so the verbose tool-progress lane was silently dropped regardless of how the destination was configured. The renderer now honors the same callbacks the native compositor honors, gated on the destination account's own config and reusing the shared `buildChannelProgressDraftLineForEntry` / `formatChannelProgressDraftText` / `mergeChannelProgressDraftLine` primitives: in `progress` stream mode with `preview.toolProgress`, the tool-progress lane renders (held for the turn, collapsed to the answer at finalize — matching native progress rendering); in `partial` mode the answer streams alone, as a native group reply would. No toggle — the mirror rides whatever that channel would do. This revision also fixes a latent bug so the native path engages for **non- channel-origin** turns (webchat `chat.send`, cron/heartbeat): the fan-out derived its origin from the session's `last*`, which those turns deliberately do not claim — so the origin defaulted to the previously-active channel and, when that channel was itself the pinned target, the fan-out **self-excluded** it and fell back to the flat post-hoc mirror. It now derives the fan-out origin from the turn's **actual** origin (`OriginatingChannel/To`), falling back to `last*`, so a webchat trigger renders natively in the pinned channel. ### New in this revision (4 commits on top of the existing channel-echo work) - `feat(reply): add /pin (pin-from-here) in-chat command` — `commands-pin.ts` + registration; stores the caller's origin identity into the bound session's echo targets. Unit tests incl. an end-to-end check chaining `/pin` through the real `resolveEchoTargets` fan-out (sibling included, self excluded). - `feat(echo): reject arbitrary-target echo adds; require a session participant` — `targetMatchesSessionParticipant` in `echo.ts`, wired into both operator add paths. Unit tests for accept/reject/fail-closed. - `fix(echo): native streaming fan-out uses the turn's actual origin, not stale last*` — `agent-runner-execution.ts`. - `feat(telegram): echo renderer rides the destination's streaming config` — `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer.ts` (+ `echo-renderer-register.ts` threading the account config/stream mode). Wires the tool-progress lane (`onToolStart`/`onItemEvent`/`onPlanUpdate`/`onCommandOutput`/`onPatchSummary`/ `onApprovalEvent`) the resolver already drives but the renderer ignored; fully contained to the Telegram extension (no core change). New unit tests assert the lane is wired in `progress` mode and absent in `partial` mode. The existing channel-echo infrastructure (native streaming fan-out, mirror resolver, owner-bound `registerEchoRendererFactory`, the types-only `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath) is otherwise unchanged. ### Tests + CI (current head `0388fad1115`) - `pnpm tsgo:core` + `tsgo:extensions`: 0 errors on the changed surface. `oxlint` on the changed files: 0 warnings / 0 errors. - Unit tests for the changed surface pass, including the **152-test `agent-runner-execution.test`** suite the origin fix touches, plus `commands-pin.test`, `echo.test`, `chat.send-user-echo.test`, and the echo suite (`echo-renderer.test` / `echo-renderer-register.test` / `echo-streaming.test`, 48 tests) — the new echo-renderer tests assert the tool-progress lane is wired in `progress` mode and absent in `partial` mode. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow with a local patched crabbox: a real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch (`OpenClaw 2026.5.31 d6c154c`, global-installed) + Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, run locally — driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with `/verbose on` and an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread and webchat are bound to one session; the thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt, and the pinned thread receives that webchat-origin turn — including the real tool result — rendered natively. Captured sequence (real Telegram Desktop, see image/video below): 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Run the shell command: date -u — use your exec tool, then tell me the UTC time…` — the webchat-origin, tool-using prompt 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The current UTC time is 14:35:12 on Thursday, June 11, 2026."** — i.e. a real `date -u` exec, run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread. A real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` turn (with `/verbose on` and an exec `date -u` tool call) is triggered from webchat and mirrored natively into the pinned Telegram thread — autoplay GIF (left, trimmed to the reply landing) + final frame (right):
Native-path engagement on the same build was confirmed by instrumenting the fan-out: for the webchat-origin turn `resolveEchoTargets` returns the Telegram target (origin = webchat, not self-excluded), the factory is found, and the renderer is **created** (native). Gateway log for the filmed run: `session turn created … channel=webchat` → the real claude turn ran the exec tool (`outcome=completed`) and the result rendered in the pinned thread. Session store after `/pin`: `echoTargets: [{"channel":"telegram","to":"telegram:-1003900553563","threadId":1,"label":"pinned"}]`. #### Riding the destination config — the tool-progress lane renders in the mirror With the destination thread in `progress` stream mode (`/verbose on`, `preview.toolProgress`), the mirror renders the **verbose tool-progress lane**, not just the answer. A real `claude-cli` turn triggered from webchat runs an exec tool (`sleep 4; hostname`) — chosen so the result is not derivable from context, proving a genuine tool call — and the pinned Telegram thread shows the **`🛠️ Bash`** progress lane while the tool runs, then collapses to the answer **`The hostname is crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`** (the real box hostname). Autoplay GIF of the full `prompt → 🛠️ Bash lane → answer` sequence (left) and the tool-lane frame (right):
**Proof environment:** single-node local gateway, one Telegram backend, isolated state dir; real `claude-cli` model with exec tools enabled. Sensitive chat ids are the test supergroup, not production. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). Implementation, tests, and the local behavior-proof capture were AI-assisted; framing and the proof are the author's.

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- opt `google-gemini-cli` into explicit OpenClaw auth-profile forwarding
- refresh/load the selected OAuth profile before CLI backend preparation
- materialize selected Gemini OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json` for the `gemini` subprocess
- keep Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Proof

Source-level behavior proof: `/tmp/openclaw-pr88742-proof.log`

- `extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts` proves selected OAuth credentials produce an isolated Gemini CLI home and writes `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` without logging token material.
- `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts` proves canonical `google/*` models routed through `google-gemini-cli` select the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile before `runCliAgent`.

Not tested:

## Real behavior proof

- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, PR branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, head `ada77084fb0bce5ccb88ba88b38ed803b5b9f0c6`, OpenClaw `2026.5.31 (7150977)`, `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`. Token values and account identifiers were not printed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Cleanup removed the isolated home afterward.
- **What was not tested**: The proof intentionally did not print raw Google OAuth access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, or private account data. The subprocess proof used `gemini --version` to prove the real Gemini CLI binary starts under the prepared `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`.

## ClawSweeper follow-up

- Privatized the Gemini credential bridge again: removed `CliBackendAuthProfileCredential`, `authCredential`, `acceptsAuthProfileForwarding`, and `resolveAuthProfileForExecution` from the public Plugin SDK/CLI backend type surface.
- Kept the selected-profile bridge scoped to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` runtime path, where the runner passes the credential through a private extension context only after selecting/refreshing the OpenClaw profile.
- Verified no public references remain:

```text

# no matches
```

### Authenticated Gemini prompt proof after bridge privacy fix

Redacted log: `/tmp/pr88748-authenticated-gemini-prompt.redacted.log`

target.title: feat(web-search): add SerpApi plugin with web search provider and 25+ specialized tools
target.body:
Add SerpApi plugin with web search provider and 31 specialized tools

## Summary

- OpenClaw had no SerpApi integration — agents could not use SerpApi's 100+ search engines or its specialized search verticals (flights, hotels, shopping, maps, YouTube, scholar, finance, news, etc.).
- Adds a new `serpapi` plugin that registers as a `web_search` provider (display name: **SerpApi Search**) and exposes 31 dedicated tools covering Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo, Yahoo, YouTube, Maps, Scholar, Finance, Flights, Hotels, Shopping, News, Events, Jobs, Amazon, eBay, Walmart, Tripadvisor, Trends, Autocomplete, Lens, AI Overview, Facebook/Instagram profiles, and Weather.
- Plugin id: `serpapi`; config: `plugins.entries.serpapi.*`; API key: `webSearch.apiKey` config or `SERPAPI_API_KEY` env var.
- Cache, timeout, language, and result count are configurable via plugin config.
- No changes to core or other plugins; uses existing `provider-web-search` SDK helpers.

### Tools

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `serpapi_ai_overview` | Fetch the full Google AI Overview for a search using a page_token |
| `serpapi_amazon` | Search Amazon for products — titles, prices, ratings, Prime status, ASIN |
| `serpapi_amazon_product` | Fetch detailed Amazon product info by ASIN |
| `serpapi_autocomplete` | Google search autocomplete suggestions for a partial query |
| `serpapi_bing` | Web search via Bing — titles, URLs, snippets |
| `serpapi_duckduckgo` | Web search via DuckDuckGo — organic results, knowledge graph, news |
| `serpapi_ebay` | Search eBay listings — titles, prices, conditions, links |
| `serpapi_ebay_product` | Detailed eBay product info by product ID |
| `serpapi_events` | Google Events search — title, date, address, ticket links |
| `serpapi_facebook_profile` | Fetch a public Facebook profile |
| `serpapi_finance` | Stock prices, currency rates, and crypto via Google Finance |
| `serpapi_flights` | Real flight prices and itineraries via Google Flights |
| `serpapi_hotels` | Hotels and accommodation via Google Hotels — price, rating, amenities |
| `serpapi_immersive_product` | Detailed Google product info by page_token from Shopping results |
| `serpapi_instagram_profile` | Fetch a public Instagram profile |
| `serpapi_jobs` | Google Jobs search — titles, companies, locations, salary |
| `serpapi_lens` | Google Lens reverse image search |
| `serpapi_maps` | Local businesses, restaurants, POIs via Google Maps |
| `serpapi_maps_reviews` | Reviews for a place on Google Maps |
| `serpapi_news` | Recent articles via Google News — headlines, sources, dates, URLs |
| `serpapi_scholar` | Academic papers, research articles, and citations via Google Scholar |
| `serpapi_shopping` | Google Shopping — product names, prices, stores, ratings |
| `serpapi_tripadvisor` | Destinations, hotels, restaurants, and attractions via Tripadvisor |
| `serpapi_trends` | Google Trends — interest over time, by region, related topics/queries |
| `serpapi_walmart` | Walmart product listings — titles, prices, ratings, links |
| `serpapi_walmart_product` | Detailed Walmart product info by product ID |
| `serpapi_weather` | Current weather or forecast for a location via Google |
| `serpapi_yahoo` | Web search via Yahoo! — titles, URLs, snippets |
| `serpapi_youtube` | YouTube search — videos, channels, shorts with metadata |
| `serpapi_youtube_transcript` | Fetch the transcript of a YouTube video |
| `serpapi_youtube_video` | YouTube video metadata — title, description, channel, views, chapters |

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: no SerpApi integration existed; this PR adds the full plugin.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test extensions/serpapi`
- Evidence after fix:
  ```
   ✓  extensions/serpapi/src/serpapi-client.test.ts (11 tests) 53ms
   ✓  extensions/serpapi/src/serpapi-search-provider.test.ts (7 tests) 248ms

   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  18 passed (18)
     Duration  590ms
  ```
- Observed result after fix: all 18 tests pass across the client and web search provider.
- What was not tested: full cross-platform validation, automated CI live API calls.
- Proof limitations: automated CI live API calls not run; live behavior validated manually via Docker with a real SerpApi key.

## Tests and validation

- Added `serpapi-client.test.ts` — HTTP client, error handling, cache behavior, missing-key error.
- Added `serpapi-search-provider.test.ts` — provider registration, credential path, and config application.
- New plugin addition; no prior behavior to regress against.

## Risk checklist

- [x] User-visible behavior changed — new plugin, tools, and web search provider available when enabled.
- [x] Config/environment changed — new `plugins.entries.serpapi.*`; new `SERPAPI_API_KEY` env var.
- [x] Security/auth/network changed — new outbound HTTPS to `serpapi.com`, gated behind API key; key never logged.

target.title: fix(agents): normalize mangled tool names and IDs from OpenAI-compati…
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Some OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. Kimi) send tool call IDs like `functions.exec:0` as `functions exec:0` (space instead of dot) and tool names like `exec` as `functions exec`. Tool result matching fails, causing "Tool functions exec not found" errors.
- **Why it matters:** Users on Kimi and similar providers cannot complete tool-using flows; matching is done by ID and name, and the mangled forms did not match.
- **What changed:** Added `normalizeMangledToolCallId()` in `tool-call-id.ts` to rewrite `functions ` -> `functions.` in IDs; use it in `extractToolResultId()` and as the key in `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist()` so mangled and canonical IDs map to the same sanitized value. Added `normalizePrefixedToolName()` in `session-transcript-repair.ts` to strip `functions.` / `functions ` from tool names; applied in assistant block handling, `hasToolCallName`, and `normalizeToolResultName()` so tool-result names are normalized and pairing works.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No change to provider clients or wire format; only transcript repair and ID sanitization for matching.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39091
- Related: normalizeToolResultName fix included

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Tool-using flows with OpenAI-compatible providers that mangle tool IDs/names (e.g. Kimi) now match tool results to tool calls correctly; "Tool functions exec not found" is resolved.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Provider: OpenAI-compatible (e.g. Kimi) that sends `functions exec:0` / `functions exec` style IDs and names

### Steps

1. Run agent with such a provider; trigger a tool call (e.g. exec).
2. Before fix: tool result may not match, "Tool functions exec not found" or similar.
3. After fix: IDs and names normalize; pairing and sanitization map mangled and canonical forms to the same value.

### Expected

Tool results pair with assistant tool calls; no spurious "tool not found" from ID/name mangling.

### Actual

As expected after the fix.

## Evidence

- `normalizeMangledToolCallId` and `extractToolResultId` / `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist` tests in `tool-call-id.test.ts`.
- `sanitizeToolUseResultPairing` test with mangled ID and prefixed name in `session-transcript-repair.test.ts`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Unit tests added; logic follows issue and Greptile feedback (compare original rawToolName in normalizeToolResultName, use normalized ID as map key).
- **Edge cases checked:** Empty string, already-canonical IDs, names without prefix.
- **What you did not verify:** Live Kimi or other provider E2E.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert the commit.
- Files: `src/agents/tool-call-id.ts`, `src/agents/session-transcript-repair.ts`.

## Risks and Mitigations

**None.** Normalization is additive and only affects matching/sanitization; no new execution paths.

target.title: fix: resolve managed SecretRef provider auth
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #92097.

This teaches provider auth resolution to bridge managed/non-env SecretRef provider `apiKey` entries through the active runtime config snapshot. The source config can keep the managed SecretRef marker/object, while runtime auth uses the already-resolved literal key from the matching runtime snapshot.

The change is intentionally narrow:

- only applies when the source provider entry has a managed non-env SecretRef marker/object
- only trusts a runtime snapshot when it matches the active source snapshot
- does not make marker-only source config work without a runtime snapshot
- keeps explicit provider api-key config ahead of ambient profile/env auth
- blocks plugin synthetic local auth from shadowing an explicit managed provider SecretRef

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/model-auth.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` — 64 passed
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` — clean, overall patch correct
- `git diff --check -- src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` — core and coreTests only

Live E2E/proof from Crabbox on this branch before the final synthetic-shadowing regression hardening:

- provider: `aws`

- run: `run_57dd3b102653`
- positive SecretRef-backed custom provider reached fake upstream with Authorization present
- marker-only source config failed before upstream auth, preserving the intended invalid-config behavior

Known proof gap: `pnpm check:changed` delegated to Blacksmith Testbox but remained queued; I stopped our queued run `tbx_01ktvv2bwe5y5psshpae92fj2w` rather than leaving it pending.

target.title: Fix suppressed heartbeat commitment delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91948.

- Treat durable `suppressed` heartbeat sends as non-visible delivery, not successful commitment delivery.
- Keep due commitments pending and avoid writing heartbeat dedupe state when the durable send is suppressed.
- Emit a skipped heartbeat event with the durable suppression reason, then allow a later visible heartbeat to retry and mark the commitment sent.
- Add a regression test covering a suppressed `No channel reply.` heartbeat followed by a visible retry.

## Root Cause

`runHeartbeatOnce` called `sendDurableMessageBatch`, threw only for failed/partial failed statuses, and then treated every other result as delivered. That meant a `suppressed` durable result from outbound planning could mark inferred commitments `sent`, write `lastHeartbeatText`/`lastHeartbeatSentAt`, and emit a `sent` heartbeat event even though no channel message was visible.

## Verification

- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean; no accepted/actionable findings reported; overall patch correct `(0.86)`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/heartbeat-runner.commitments.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` - 10 tests passed after rebase.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/message/send.test.ts src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/route-reply.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` - 54 tests passed.
- Regression repro: with only the runtime fix temporarily reverted, the new focused test failed on current behavior with `expected 'sent' to deeply equal 'pending'`; after restoring the fix, the focused test passed.
- Live AWS Crabbox proof, run `run_6ac6aacdd5f2`: first suppressed Telegram heartbeat left the commitment `pending`, kept `sentAtMs` unset, did not write heartbeat dedupe state, and emitted `skipped` with `reason: no_visible_payload`; the next visible heartbeat sent exactly one Telegram message and marked the commitment `sent`.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: Fail closed for CLI-backed /btw fallback
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #92168.

- fail closed when `/btw` would otherwise fall back to direct provider auth for a model routed through a CLI runtime alias
- preserve existing `/btw` direct fallback behavior for non-CLI/custom harnesses without side-question hooks
- add regression coverage proving CLI-runtime alias models do not call direct provider auth or direct streaming

## Verification

- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/btw.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/btw.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "fails closed instead of using direct provider auth for CLI-runtime alias models|keeps the direct provider fallback for non-Codex harnesses without side-question hooks"`
- `git diff --check -- src/agents/btw.ts src/agents/btw.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `/btw` no longer silently switches a CLI-runtime-backed model to direct provider auth/streaming when no side-question hook is available.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node/Vitest agent test lane.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/btw.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` and the focused regression command listed above.

Evidence after fix: full `src/agents/btw.test.ts` passed 34/34; focused regression proof passed 2/2.

Observed result after fix: CLI-runtime alias models fail closed before `getApiKeyForModel`, provider stream registration, or direct `streamSimple`; non-CLI harness fallback remains available.

What was not tested: no live Crabbox run was repeated after the patch; the fix is covered by targeted unit regression proof for the implicated `/btw` fallback path.
